content.text: Shipping an AI agent to real users without first validating it in controlled, progressively higher-fidelity environments is one of the fastest ways to damage user trust and incur unexpected LLM costs. The solution is a three-tier environment model — development, staging, and production — each with a distinct purpose, configuration, and quality bar. This model predates AI, but the nondeterministic, cost-sensitive nature of LLM-backed agents makes its disciplined application more consequential than in conventional software.
What is the Dev/Staging/Prod environment model?

The dev/staging/prod model is a deployment discipline in which every version of a service must pass through a sequence of progressively production-like environments before reaching end users. Each environment is an independently isolated copy of the system — its own infrastructure, configuration, secrets, and (often) data — so that problems detected at one tier stay contained and can be fixed before advancing to the next.
— the engineer's local or personal cloud workspace. Fastest iteration cycle; mock external services; verbose logging; no SLA; full debug tooling.

— a shared, stable copy of production. Uses real (or anonymised real) data, real LLM API keys with budget caps, and a full evaluation suite. Quality assurance, stakeholder review, and load testing happen here.
— the live system serving real users. Full monitoring, alerting, on-call rotation, SLA enforcement, and rollback capability. Only artifacts promoted through staging are deployed here.

For agentic AI specifically, each tier adds an additional dimension: the fidelity of LLM calls. In dev, most teams replace real LLM calls with recorded fixtures or cheap stub models so that iteration is free and deterministic. In staging, real model APIs are called but under hard spend caps and against a narrow set of golden evaluation prompts. In production, the full model is live, costs are charged to the product account, and every call is traced.

Without environment separation, any code change goes straight to production. For a conventional CRUD API this is reckless; for an AI agent it is doubly dangerous. Agent behaviour is not fully captured by unit tests because the model itself is an opaque function that can change its response to the same prompt across API versions, system-prompt edits, or retrieval-index updates. The only reliable way to validate behaviour is to observe the agent processing realistic inputs — which requires an environment that faithfully mirrors production.
Concretely, environment separation prevents several classes of production incident that are otherwise difficult to catch:
— a reworded system prompt that worked in isolation causes the agent to hallucinate tool arguments in realistic multi-turn sessions.
— a new agent loop that looked cheap in dev with mocked responses turns out to make 40 LLM calls per user request on real inputs.
— a database URL, API key, or feature flag that was manually set in prod but never codified causes deployment failures and hard-to-reproduce bugs.
— changes to tool permissions or guardrails that appeared correct in dev allow unintended actions in prod-scale session volume.
— a broken deployment in staging affects no real users and can be rolled back in minutes without SLA impact.
— the same container image tagged with a git SHA is promoted unchanged through all three tiers, eliminating "works on my machine" drift.
— every production deployment is traceable to the exact commit, eval results, and approval record that authorised it.
— staging spend caps prevent runaway token usage from reaching the production billing account.
— dev environments with mocked LLM responses let engineers iterate on agent logic at near-zero cost and sub-second latency.
— multiple feature branches can be deployed to independent dev environments simultaneously without blocking each other.

Environment separation introduces real operational overhead. Running three tiers means paying for infrastructure, secrets management, and monitoring in each. For very small teams this cost can outweigh the benefit — a one-person side project may reasonably skip a formal staging environment early in development. The threshold shifts once a service has paying users or processes sensitive data, at which point the cost of a production incident easily exceeds the cost of staging infrastructure.

— staging environments that use anonymised or synthetic data miss bugs that only surface on the real distribution of prod inputs. This is especially acute for agents that rely on RAG over a live knowledge base.
— keeping staging databases in sync with production without leaking PII requires explicit data-masking pipelines that add engineering effort.
— mock LLM responses that were recorded weeks ago may no longer match real model output if the API or the agent's prompts have changed, leading to false confidence in dev.
— a three-tier promotion pipeline adds hours or days to the release cycle compared to direct-to-prod deployment, which can slow hotfix delivery.

The model works by separating configuration from code. The application binary or container image is identical across all three environments; what changes is the configuration injected at runtime — environment variables, secrets, feature flags, and infrastructure endpoints. This separation is enforced by the twelve-factor app principle: configuration lives in the environment, never in the committed codebase.

A typical promotion flow works as follows. A developer writes code, runs the agent locally against mocked LLM responses, and opens a pull request. The CI system builds an immutable container image tagged with the commit SHA and runs fast quality gates: linting, type checking, unit tests, and a brief smoke evaluation using cheap or recorded LLM responses. On merge to the main branch, the pipeline automatically deploys this image to staging. In staging, the full evaluation suite runs against real model APIs under a spend cap; a QA engineer or automated approval gate reviews the results. If the gate passes, a promotion event deploys the same image to production — no rebuild, no source change, just a new config pointing at the prod infrastructure.
Configuration isolation is typically achieved through a hierarchy of environment variable sources. A base

file (never committed to version control) provides developer-local overrides. CI pipelines inject staging and production values through secrets management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GitHub Actions Secrets). The application reads configuration through a single entry point — a settings module — that validates all required variables at startup and raises a clear error if any are missing, rather than letting the service start with silent misconfigurations.
For LLM-backed agents, an additional layer of configuration controls model fidelity per environment. A
flag in dev routes all LLM calls to a local fixture server that replays pre-recorded responses. A
variable in staging aborts the eval run if accumulated API cost exceeds the threshold. In production, both flags are absent and full observability is enabled.
The diagram below shows the three-environment pipeline for an agentic AI service, from developer workstation through CI/CD to production, including the promotion gates and rollback path.

attrs.alt: Dev / Staging / Production environment pipeline showing three environment boxes with their characteristics and the CI/CD stages connecting them

content.text: Each environment box in the diagram carries a distinct

value that all application code reads to select its behaviour. The CI/CD pipeline (bottom bar) runs a chain of quality gates — build, unit tests, eval suite, staging deploy, approval gate, and prod deploy — and the purple rollback arrow shows that a production incident can revert to the last known-good staging image without rebuilding.
A full three-tier environment model is appropriate when:
The service has real users whose experience would be harmed by a bad deployment.
LLM API costs are material and a runaway prompt change could generate unexpected bills.
The agent accesses sensitive data (PII, financial records, code repositories) where a misconfiguration in prod would have compliance or legal consequences.
The team has more than one engineer contributing to the same codebase, making coordinated promotion gates necessary.
The agent executes consequential real-world actions (sending emails, making API calls, modifying databases) where a bad deployment would create irreversible side effects.

Skip a formal staging tier when the project is a personal prototype with zero external users and no sensitive data, or when the cost of staging infrastructure significantly exceeds the cost of an occasional bad deployment that can be rolled back manually in seconds.
that reads all configuration from environment variables and validates them at import time. Use Pydantic Settings or a similar library so missing values produce a clear startup error.
(real prod secrets live in a secrets manager, not git). Add all real env files to
— wrap the LLM client with a thin adapter that checks
and serves fixture responses from a local JSON file instead of making real API calls.
that runs on every pull request: build image, lint, unit tests, optional smoke eval, tag image with git SHA, push to registry.
triggered by merges to main. Inject staging secrets from the secrets manager. Run the full eval suite against staging; fail the deployment if any eval drops below threshold.
— either a manual approval step in the CD system (GitHub Environments, GitLab protected environments) or an automated pass/fail check on eval metrics, spend, and latency.
by promoting the same container image (same SHA) that passed staging. Use a blue-green or canary strategy to limit user exposure during the rollout window.
— ensure the CD system can redeploy the previous image in under five minutes, and document the rollback command in the runbook.
Example 1: Pydantic Settings with environment-aware LLM client

# config.py
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict
from typing import Literal


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
        env_file=".env",
        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
    )

    # Deployment tier
    env: Literal["development", "staging", "production"] = "development"

    # LLM settings
    mock_llm: bool = False
    llm_model: str = "gpt-4o-mini"
    llm_spend_cap_usd: float | None = None
    openai_api_key: str = ""

    # Database
    database_url: str = "sqlite:///./dev.db"

    # Agent feature flags
    enable_web_search: bool = False
    enable_code_execution: bool = False

    @property
    def is_dev(self) -> bool:
        return self.env == "development"

    @property
    def is_prod(self) -> bool:
        return self.env == "production"


settings = Settings()


# agent/llm_client.py
from openai import OpenAI
import json
from pathlib import Path
from config import settings


class LLMClient:
    """Thin adapter that serves mock responses in dev."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._fixtures: dict | None = None
        if settings.mock_llm:
            fixtures_path = Path("tests/fixtures/llm_responses.json")
            self._fixtures = json.loads(fixtures_path.read_text())
        else:
            self._client = OpenAI(api_key=settings.openai_api_key)

    def complete(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> str:
        if self._fixtures is not None:
            last_user = next(
                (m["content"] for m in reversed(messages) if m["role"] == "user"),
                "",
            )
            for key, response in self._fixtures.items():
                if key in last_user:
                    return response
            return self._fixtures.get("__default__", "Mock response.")

        response = self._client.chat.completions.create(
            model=settings.llm_model,
            messages=messages,
            **kwargs,
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.content or ""

# .env.dev  (committed as .env.dev.example, real file in .gitignore)
ENV=development
MOCK_LLM=true
LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-fake-dev-key
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///./dev.db
ENABLE_WEB_SEARCH=false
ENABLE_CODE_EXECUTION=false

# .env.staging
ENV=staging
MOCK_LLM=false
LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini
# OPENAI_API_KEY injected by CI/CD from secrets manager
LLM_SPEND_CAP_USD=10.0
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://staging-db.internal:5432/agentdb
ENABLE_WEB_SEARCH=true
ENABLE_CODE_EXECUTION=false

# .env.prod.example  (real values never committed)
ENV=production
MOCK_LLM=false
LLM_MODEL=gpt-4o
# OPENAI_API_KEY injected at deploy time from AWS Secrets Manager
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://prod-db.internal:5432/agentdb
ENABLE_WEB_SEARCH=true
ENABLE_CODE_EXECUTION=true
Example 3: GitHub Actions workflow for three-tier promotion

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Build and Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/agent

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build image
        run: docker build -t $IMAGE:${{ github.sha }} .

      - name: Unit tests (mock LLM)
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -e ENV=development \
            -e MOCK_LLM=true \
            $IMAGE:${{ github.sha }} \
            python -m pytest tests/unit/ -q

      - name: Push to registry
        if: github.event_name == 'push'
        run: |
          echo ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} | docker login ghcr.io -u $ --password-stdin
          docker push $IMAGE:${{ github.sha }}

  deploy-staging:
    needs: build-and-test
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent \
            agent=$IMAGE:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace=staging
          kubectl rollout status deployment/agent --namespace=staging --timeout=3m

      - name: Run eval suite
        env:
          STAGING_URL: ${{ vars.STAGING_URL }}
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.STAGING_OPENAI_KEY }}
        run: |
          python scripts/run_evals.py \
            --base-url $STAGING_URL \
            --threshold 0.90 \
            --spend-cap 10.0

  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - name: Promote to production
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent \
            agent=$IMAGE:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace=production
          kubectl rollout status deployment/agent --namespace=production --timeout=5m

Consider a customer-support agent that reads from a CRM, searches a knowledge base, and sends email replies on behalf of support staff. The agent runs on GPT-4o, handles sensitive customer PII, and processes roughly 2,000 automated tickets per day. A broken deployment that causes the agent to misclassify tickets or send the wrong template to customers would be immediately visible and potentially a compliance issue.

, engineers run the agent locally against a SQLite copy of three anonymised customer records and a fixture file containing pre-recorded GPT-4o responses for each intent. All email sending is disabled. In

, the agent runs against a PostgreSQL snapshot refreshed weekly from production with all name and email fields replaced by fake values. Real GPT-4o-mini calls are made (downgraded to keep costs low) with a $10 spend cap per deploy. Email sending calls a staging inbox, not the real SendGrid account. A suite of 150 golden test cases checks intent classification accuracy (threshold: 92%). In
, GPT-4o is used, all actions are live, and every session is traced. Canary deployments roll out to 5% of traffic for 30 minutes before full promotion.

# scripts/run_evals.py
import argparse
import httpx
import json
from pathlib import Path


def run_evals(
    base_url: str,
    threshold: float = 0.90,
    spend_cap: float | None = None,
) -> None:
    golden_cases = json.loads(Path("evals/golden_cases.json").read_text())
    passed = 0
    total_cost_usd = 0.0

    with httpx.Client(base_url=base_url, timeout=30) as client:
        for case in golden_cases:
            resp = client.post("/agent/run", json={"ticket": case["input"]})
            resp.raise_for_status()
            result = resp.json()

            if result["intent"] == case["expected_intent"]:
                passed += 1

            total_cost_usd += result.get("cost_usd", 0.0)

            if spend_cap and total_cost_usd > spend_cap:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    f"Eval aborted: spend cap ${spend_cap} exceeded "
                    f"(actual: ${total_cost_usd:.2f})"
                )

    accuracy = passed / len(golden_cases)
    print(f"Eval accuracy: {accuracy:.1%}  ({passed}/{len(golden_cases)})")
    print(f"Total eval cost: ${total_cost_usd:.4f}")

    if accuracy < threshold:
        raise SystemExit(
            f"EVAL FAILED: accuracy {accuracy:.1%} < threshold {threshold:.1%}"
        )
    print("EVAL PASSED")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--base-url", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--threshold", type=float, default=0.90)
    parser.add_argument("--spend-cap", type=float, default=None)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    run_evals(args.base_url, args.threshold, args.spend_cap)
The most common and damaging mistake is committing API keys, database passwords, or service account credentials inside
. GitHub scans public repositories and revokes leaked OpenAI keys automatically, but private repositories with access credentials are still exposed to anyone with repo read access.

# WRONG: .env with real secrets committed to git
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-proj-abcdefg123456...
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://admin:supersecret@prod.db.internal/app

# RIGHT: .env.example committed; real .env in .gitignore
# .env.example
OPENAI_API_KEY=<injected by CI from secrets manager>
DATABASE_URL=<injected by CI from secrets manager>

# .gitignore additions
.env
.env.dev
.env.staging
# Keep .env.*.example committed as documentation

A frequent mistake is rebuilding the Docker image in each environment rather than promoting the same image. This breaks the guarantee that what was tested in staging is what runs in production, because the build environment, dependency resolver, or package registry may produce a subtly different result.

# WRONG: rebuild on deploy (different result each time)
kubectl set image deployment/agent agent=myregistry/agent:latest

# RIGHT: promote the exact SHA that passed staging
kubectl set image deployment/agent \
  agent=myregistry/agent:a3f8b21c7d91e4560f2a8bcd01234567890abcde

Teams sometimes mock the LLM in staging as well as dev, defeating the purpose of the staging tier. Staging must exercise real LLM calls with realistic inputs; otherwise the first time the model sees production-scale prompt diversity is in production itself. The spend cap exists precisely to make real staging calls affordable without risking a runaway bill.

SSH-ing into a production instance to tweak a config value or restart a service is a common shortcut that creates invisible state. The next deployment overwrites the change without warning, creating mysterious "worked yesterday, broken today" incidents. All configuration must flow through the CD pipeline, even for hotfixes.
— all environment variable names and their expected types should be documented in
and validated by the settings module at startup.
Pin dependency versions in the container image
) to ensure the image is reproducible across builds.
Match staging infrastructure to production as closely as budget allows
— same database engine version, same vector store, same message broker. Version differences in middleware are a common source of staging-prod divergence.
— run a nightly CI job that refreshes
by calling the real API, so dev fixtures stay current with actual model behaviour.
Set hard LLM spend caps in staging
— implement at both the application level (abort eval if cap exceeded) and the LLM provider level (account soft limit) to prevent runaway costs.
Never skip the promotion gate under time pressure
— document a lightweight emergency path (abbreviated eval on key golden tests) that still validates the change, even during urgency.
Label every release artifact with traceability metadata
— Docker image labels should include the git SHA, branch, build timestamp, and the eval score that cleared promotion, making incident root-cause analysis a label lookup.
The environment model itself does not directly constrain performance, but several interaction effects matter at scale.

Staging infrastructure costs scale with fidelity. Running staging at exactly production scale is the highest-fidelity option but doubles the infrastructure bill. A common compromise is to run staging at 20–30% of production capacity, sufficient to catch most correctness issues. Staging-specific autoscaling limits (
Eval suite runtime is a CI bottleneck. A suite of 500 golden cases against GPT-4o-mini typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on concurrency and rate limits. Strategies to keep the pipeline fast:
Split evals into a fast smoke set (10–20 cases, runs on every PR) and a comprehensive golden set (all cases, runs on staging deploy only).
Parallelise eval workers: send requests concurrently up to the LLM provider's rate limit (typically 60–500 RPM depending on tier).
Cache LLM responses for deterministic eval cases using provider-side prompt caching, reducing both latency and cost for repeated prompts.

Database migration ordering is a scalability concern at the staging-to-production boundary. Running schema migrations before deploying the new application image requires backward-compatible migrations — the old application image must work against the new schema during the rolling update window. For AI agents that store session state, conversation history, or embedding vectors, this constraint should inform schema design from the start: additive-only migrations and versioned schema changes avoid needing a coordinated hard cutover.
Build images, run tests, deploy to staging and prod; GitHub Environments provides manual approval gates
Reads and validates env vars at startup; type-safe, self-documenting settings class
Stores LLM API keys and DB credentials; injects them as env vars at deploy time without committing to git
Immutable images tagged with git SHA; Kubernetes namespaces map to dev/staging/prod; configmaps and secrets per namespace
Codifies env-specific infra (instance sizes, replica counts, VPC peering) as workspace-parameterised modules
Traces LLM calls and agent sessions; separates staging vs prod data by project or environment tag
Loads .env files in dev; pydantic-settings wraps this automatically in the settings class
Enables per-environment or per-user rollout of new agent capabilities without a code deploy
Production agent services with real users and sensitive data
Small teams with fast rollback and low user impact tolerance
Incremental feature rollout within an already-deployed image
High-velocity teams with strong real-time monitoring and fast automated rollback

Environment isolation is itself a security boundary. Each tier should run in a separate cloud account, Kubernetes namespace, or at minimum a separate VPC, so that a compromised staging workload cannot read production database credentials or LLM API keys. Shared secrets between environments are a common source of security incidents: a developer who accidentally triggers a destructive action in staging should be unable to reach production infrastructure.
— define and test a maximum rollback time (e.g. 5 minutes) and include it in the on-call runbook. Rollback should be a single command with no manual image rebuilding.
— never SSH into a running instance to patch code or config. All changes flow through the CI/CD pipeline to maintain full auditability and reproducibility.
— LLM API keys and database credentials should be rotatable without a code deploy. The application should re-read secrets from the secrets manager on startup rather than caching them in memory.
Separate LLM API keys per environment

— use different keys for dev, staging, and prod. This enables per-environment spend tracking, allows the staging key to be revoked without affecting prod, and isolates usage data for billing attribution.

— anonymise or synthesise production data before loading it into staging. Use a data masking pipeline that replaces PII fields (names, emails, phone numbers) with realistic but fake values while preserving distribution characteristics that matter for eval quality.

— restrict agent tool access to allowlisted external APIs. This prevents a misconfigured agent from sending real emails, posting to external services, or making billable third-party API calls during automated evals.
The dev/staging/prod model separates code from configuration: the same immutable container image is promoted through all three tiers; only environment variables and secrets change between them.

For agentic AI, each environment tier adds a fidelity dimension for LLM calls: mocked in dev for fast free iteration, real with spend caps in staging for behaviour validation, fully live in production.
Staging must exercise real LLM calls against realistic inputs; mocking the LLM in staging defeats its purpose and defers all behaviour risk to production.
Secrets must never be committed to version control; they belong in a secrets manager injected at deploy time, with separate API keys per environment for spend isolation and revocability.
A promotion gate between staging and production — either a manual approval or an automated eval threshold check — is the primary mechanism to prevent behaviour regressions from reaching users.
Rollback must be a single command that redeploys the previous SHA in under five minutes; never rebuild from source during an incident.
Production data used in staging requires anonymisation, and staging network egress should be restricted to prevent accidental real-world side effects during automated eval runs.
